items.q: What is the difference between glucose and sucrose?
items.correct: Glucose is a monosaccharide (single sugar); sucrose is a disaccharide (glucose + fructose).
items.random_wrong: Glucose is artificial, sucrose is natural.
items.why: Students treat all sugars as identical. Sucrose (table sugar) is two sugars bonded together - the body splits it into glucose and fructose during digestion. Different sugars have different metabolic paths.

items.q: Which method of heat transfer cooks food in a microwave?
items.correct: Radiation — microwaves are electromagnetic radiation that makes polar water molecules vibrate, generating heat.
items.misconception: Microwaves cook food from the inside out.
items.why: Microwaves penetrate ~1–2 cm into food and excite water molecules — they cook outer regions first, conducting heat inward. The 'inside out' myth comes from feeling cool outer surfaces after microwaving (because steam carries heat out).

items.q: What does freezing do to bacteria in food?
items.correct: Makes them dormant (slows or halts growth), but does not kill them.
items.misconception: Kills all bacteria in the food.
items.why: Students think freezing sterilises food. It just stops bacteria multiplying - they revive when thawed. That's why thawed food should be cooked promptly and not refrozen raw.

items.q: Roughly how many minutes to knead?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 10. “30” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How do you tell bread is done?
items.why: Correct answer: Hollow sound when tapped. “Smells sweet” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: At roughly what temp do you bake bread (C)?
items.why: Exact answer: 200. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
